Hey, it's Dave D and welcome to Magnetic Marketing Session Number One, Secrets Behind the Awesome Power of Magnetic Marketing Revealed. I'm going to be your host and we have with us Mr. Dan Kennedy, and he's going to share with you the secrets that will change your business life forever. Now, Dan conceived Magnetic Marketing, no pressure at all right Dan? No pressure. No pressure. Dan conceived Magnetic Marketing 25 years ago for business owners with no experience and no marketing knowledge to empower them to create compelling marketing on their own without having to hire outside help. And he created a system and a toolkit for small business owners to target their ideal high value customers or clients who are ready and willing to give them money again and again. I was one of those small business owners who bought Magnetic Marketing over 17 years ago. I saw Dan speaking and I was a struggling entertainer at the time doing about three shows a month. I was thinking about having to get a real job and I invested in Magnetic Marketing. More importantly, I implemented Magnetic Marketing and I went from doing three shows a month averaging 25 shows a month in less than 90 days. And the truth is this is very exciting. This is with no improvement in your talent. I want an ace in the ad. No improvement in my talent at all. This is all due to Magnetic Marketing. But the truth is this is a big deal for me because 17 years ago I was sitting in an audience watching this man speak and now I get to be part of the creation of the new Magnetic Marketing. So Dan, thank you so much for what you've done for me and what you've done for thousands of people. And now with the new Magnetic Marketing, what you're going to do for literally tens of thousands of people in the future as well. So we're really excited about this. So let's get right into this. One of the things that you talk about is that marketing is the most important function in a business. And that's the most important function that a business person needs to master. Why is that so? Well, it actually is the business and everything else is a deliverable. So we can go beyond most important function and actually get to an understanding that the business you're in is the marketing business. And whatever it is that you exchange for money is a deliverable, which is even a higher level of understanding. But the main reason that it is so important is because nothing happens in a business until somebody is brought in the door. So if you're a veterinarian, you really don't get to be a veterinarian unless somebody walks through the door with a dog, cat, lion, parrot, rabbit, snake. And if you're a restaurant owner, you really don't get to be a restaurant owner unless somebody walks in with an appetite in a wallet. So the marketing is what allows you to do. You don't get to do a show when you were making a living as an entertainer. You don't get to perform unless there's an audience who has paid ticket prices to somebody in order for... I mean, you could go perform in an empty theater, but you won't do that very often because you'll get skinnier and skinnier and skinnier until you'll be unable to get up off the stage and do anything. So marketing is really the ante to be able to be in whatever game it is that you think you're in that actually is your deliverable. The second reason it's so important is because we live in an environment of enormous clutter and enormous commoditization, meaning there's no shortage of anything, pretty much anywhere. Maybe MDs in small rural areas. So there's an exception to every rule, but fundamentally people have more choices. There's more choices than there are people to take them in almost every category. Starbucks is, you know, the joke is they're to the point of opening Starbucks in the restrooms in Starbucks. Yeah. And so being that there's no shortage, that heightens the need for differentiation, for being a preferred choice rather than just one of many choices made at random. And that, contrary to what a lot of people believe, does not happen by excellence alone. And in some cases it doesn't happen by excellence at all. It happens by marketing. I mean, the Japanese took over the auto market in the United States at one point and they had really crappy cars, but they still took over the marketplace. So it's a truth a lot of people don't like to understand. The third reason it's most important is it's like it or not, it's where all the money is. The highest paid people, the highest paid function, the most expensive thing anybody does in business is acquire customers and sell more to the customers that they have, which is the marketing function. And often the doer, the provider of the goods and services. So like I was just in Vegas. And the last year in Las Vegas, the highest paid celebrity disc jockey working the clubs was paid $58 million, which coincidentally is identical to the gross earnings of Justin Bieber last year, just to even give you more perspective. Wow. So Bieber's gross was $58 million, a guy spinning records in nightclubs in Vegas, $58 million. What's significant is that the bartender worked for minimum wage and tips, but the bartender is providing the deliverable. The celebrity DJ is what is part and parcel of what brings them in the door. So you can't do low wage work and expect high pay. And the high pay is all tied to the ability to acquire a customer, sell to customers, keep customers, multiply customers for functions of marketing. You know, one of the things you joked about at the very beginning was I went from doing the three shows to the 25 shows and didn't improve my talent, which was true. Of course it's true. Right. One of the things that had such a profound impact. I mean, no insult intended. Yeah, yeah. Well, actually there was an insult intended. Yeah, yeah, the truth is true. Yeah, so one of the things that you said once was, and it really stuck with me, that it's more important to be a great marketer than a great doer of what you do, because that's not an advantage until somebody actually... It's not an advantage to somebody experiences. Right. It's the easier thing to hire. Right. So I had a client here yesterday who has a very large dermatology practice in New York, multi-office dermatology practice. And he is the chief expert dermatologist. And he can make scars go away and etc., etc., etc. His chief problem in life is getting enough patients through the doors to support all the docs and opening the next one and the next one and the next one. Right. Well, it's infinitely easier to hire and train up good dermatologists than it is to hire somebody and afford them. Right. Who can feed all those practices with patients. Right. And he's sitting in a room with me paying $19,400 to be there for a day to talk about marketing. He is not paying all the dermatologists at it together. Right. $19,000 today. Right. So the money is in the marketing. If you want to master something in your business that is tied to the highest compensation available, you'll master the marketing. And you'll work on improving your marketing skill more than you will work on improving your deliverable skill. Because often good enough is good enough in the deliverable category. You know, and that's the opposite of what we're taught. Right. I mean, we're taught that, hey, practice get really good at your craft. If you're the best at what you do, you're going to make the most money. Yeah. That doesn't seem fair. Well, it's probably not. Right. But justice is really... A rhythm. Well, it's really an academic conversation. But the reality is that not only are we conditioned to think in terms of getting better and better and better and better and better at the doing of the thing. So a lot of speakers, so you come from entertainer, I come from speaker. You know, they're spending their lives in seminars without breathing technique and voice and all of that. And the 10 highest paid speakers in the world would get C and D and E grades by the professionals who teach all of that stuff who incidentally make a whole lot less money than do the people that they would be most critical of. And so not only is the conditioning a problem, it's actually counterintuitive. I mean, it's very natural to think that getting better at the core skill, the core competency at the deliverable has a direct relationship to money. But it does not. And if you become very rational and logical about it, you will recognize that it does not. So in your field, if the public goes to a magic show and the elephant disappears and they are fooled, right? If there's technical flaws in that performance that only a teeny number of people, mostly people who are not giving you money anyway, they are other magicians or academic critics of magic would spot. Then you getting so much better at making the elephant disappear that all those technical flaws go away will not equate to any increase in income whatsoever. And so good enough is good enough and you focus on marketing yourself and your show, not on pleasing the most determined and diligent critic of that kind of a performance. Well, that leads right into the next question. What is the biggest mistake that business owners make with their marketing? Well, we sort of just went through one. Right. So another is copycatting the wrong stuff. So if you are a small business owner, an entrepreneur, a sales professional, some kind of solo practitioner, and you in any way model after what you see big business doing. Now, you might think that's aspirational, but you will not find any big business that grew from a small business that did things the way they're doing now. Then you won't find a single one. And in some cases, abandoning what brought them to the dance actually is harmful. But if you copy them, they and their advertising agencies and internal marketing people are basically all operating from a different agenda than you are. And their agenda is much more complicated. So some of what they do that would be very harmful to the small business owner is defensible in the situation that they are in. But their agenda is involved, in many cases, if they're public corporations, their agenda is involved Wall Street, having to please Wall Street stockholders, other kinds of investors. They may be looking for their next, we're going to go public, we're going to sell this company, we're going to merge this company, so we have to make it palatable for whoever it is that we're going to marry. Right. And so you were perfectly happy in jeans and t-shirts and laundry on the floor, but if you decide now I want to get married and settle down, you got to take a shower every morning and you got to put on better clothes. So same deal here, right? The agencies have their own agendas. How can we win awards? How can we do stuff that's so cool? The media talks about it, which attracts other big companies as clients. But the small business owner, which the room here is mostly small business owners, so the garter's mattress guys see their agenda is much simpler. How can we sell a mattress today, preferably at a good profit, to cover the checks we wrote yesterday? That's the small business agenda. It's much cleaner and less complicated. But therefore, if you copy what the big companies are doing, you're copying something being done for reasons that are not your reasons. And that's very, very dangerous. So you see, and corollary to that is, you see big businesses are really operating with play money. UI, they see they're operating with real money. Big businesses are operating with play money. Hence, you see companies like, I just wrote about it, Overstock.com. So everybody knows what Overstock.com is. And you would assume it is a big business, hundreds of millions of dollars a year. They've never made a profit, not one dollar of profit ever, right? Yet, if you want to be kind. And they don't have to, because they're operating with big bushel baskets of play money from Wall Street. Right. From venture capital firms. So they are nearly, the government is in the best position because they can legally print it. Right? So whatever they do is insane because they have an endless supply of money. A public company is almost like that. They have a nearly endless supply of money. And they don't have to make a profit. We, if we're going to cover payroll on the 15th of the month, we're dealing with real money. We actually, nobody will give us play money. Right. So if you copy the people who have access to play money, and you only have real money, you're going to be roadkill. Right. Right. And that's why you see magnetic marketing, which is, you know, an outgrowth of direct response marketing, which really has its roots in mail order marketing. You see it in a lot of startup and early stage companies. And then you see it gradually go away. But if you trace the history, if you picked a large successful company and you traced its genealogy backwards, you would find often this kind, our kind of marketing in play and then gradually erased. Ogilvy is famous, the great ad band Ogilvy for screaming at his own people, that only the director of response people really know what they're doing. Right. And so you have to ask yourself, do you want to spend your money based on knowns, on knowing what you're doing? Or do you want to spend your money based on academic theory? Or do you want to spend your money in ways that you could only evaluate the effectiveness of it, three calendar quarters from now, by market share movement, how do you want to spend your money? Right. Also, you'll find that this kind of marketing, direct response marketing, made useful for any business within the magnetic marketing format, typically gives about a three times to a 10 times boost in return on investment. And it works in every field. There's not a single industry. I didn't know those guys were coming, but we got from retail, we have Garder's mattress here. I mean, I made a list. You know, we had service businesses, Iron Tribe Fitness is kicking about and taking names. I just was with a group of dentists a couple of weeks ago, and one of our members, Donna Galante, was there who has the largest, most successful, most profitable Invisalign, the clear braces, Invisalign practice in America. In three years, she's gone from 19 competitors to 38 competitors and circles around her. She's the most expensive one of any of them, and she has double digit year to year growth. Dr. Tom Shack, who's here, you bumped into him yesterday, has been able to successfully franchise chiropractic practices because of the basis of this kind of marketing that fuels the practices. Craig Proctor in real estate, Michael Jans and Robin Robbins, and B2B, e-commerce, Spindler at kidsbullfree.com with 2 million customers. It's all the same marketing system, but it is one that a lot of big corporations actually do the opposite of. And so the first biggest mistake is copying what appears to be successful, but has a very different definition of success than what your definition of success is or needs to be. Right. So just so for the toolkit owners who already have the toolkit inside, a lot of the names that Dan just named, there's examples right inside this toolkit for what they're doing with their marketing and how they've turned it into magnetic marketing. And so make sure you look through your toolkit. So Dan, one of the things that we talk about, we keep banding about this term magnetic marketing, it's a phrase that you coined many years ago. What exactly is this magnetic marketing? What exactly is it? It is about attraction rather than push or pursuit. So it's basically three ways to sell something. One is to push it on people. In the retail store environment, aisles are constructed so that you can't get through them without bumping into this end cap display full of product. And all the way to the point that chances are you're going to bump it with the cart and two of them are going to fall in and to not buy you're going to have to take them out or your kids are going to reach as you go by and grab it. The cash register is this compilation maze of impulse items. So that's push. You have pursuit, which is what's most commonly used, which is I knock on your door and if you don't, if you don't come to the door, I go around to your back door, I come down to chimney. I'm just constantly chasing people, trying to get them to slow down and grudgingly give me their attention so that I, in a retail environment, you walk in and the clerk says, can I help you? And you reflexively say, no, I'm just looking. But then the clerk pursues you all through the store. And if they're on an up system where they got you and now they don't get the next one, they really pursue you. And so every five or 10 minutes, she or he is popping back up in your field of vision. You know, you hesitated for 12 seconds in front of the wristwatches and she has popped up pursuing you to talk to you about wristwatches. Which is so uncomfortable, Dan, not only for the customer, for the prospect, but for the person who's doing the selling. Well, it's an ugly thing. And at that level, you know, it's not so super ugly, but as you sort of move up into different environments, it's uglier and uglier and uglier. And as you point out, it's stressful for both people in the equation. And what both of those things have done, by the way, is they make it, they make a lot of people reluctant to put themselves in a, I might buy something environment. So even when they have interest, they stop short of putting themselves in that position. So I do a lot of work with financial advisors, for example, who a primary method of their marketing is to get older people, my age and above. Into a workshop, usually with the lure of food. And then give them a dog and potty show and try and schedule appointments with them to now take their money and move it around. Right. So that's inelegantly said. But so for every person now who actually comes in that room, there's 10 who did have an interest in what appeared in front of them, inviting them to come into that room. They know they have a need that is addressed by that, but they stop themselves from coming into the room because they anticipate one of these push-slash-pursuit experiences where in the room they're going to be made to feel very uncomfortable and they're going to be pressured and they're going to be him and they're going to be push, push, push, push, push, push, push, push to book that appointment. Right. And the financial advisors assisted is going to be, you know, standing between them and the door at the end as they're trying to get out. And then if they don't book an appointment, maybe somebody's going to call them every night at dinner and disrupt Wheel of Fortune for the next two weeks. So they're very reluctant to see that. You asked earlier. See, that was like funny. It wasn't laugh funny, but it was chuckle funny. We'll loosen them up. We got three breaks. We could get her out. In case you don't know it, we have a replacement audience. We have what we call a standby audience in another room. Here's the thing. We just shuffle. The houses of people watching online right now, they're in hysterics right now. They're banging on the table. Okay. So where was I? So these people are reluctant to put themselves in that position and you can't blame them. Right. Right. So when you translate that to response, so people will say, you direct mail, what response percentage do you get? Well, the reason you get such a tidy response percentage is because of this much bigger percentage who is interested, but will not put themselves into a position that they know is going to be uncomfortable and awkward and stressful and unpleasant. Right. And so the third approach is the attraction approach. And so magnetic marketing is a marriage of metaphysical concepts about attraction and magnetism. So people fairly recently, if this is new to them in any way, they may have noticed the secret. Right. The kind of burns thing that now is what, four or five years old, but you can still find a whole pile of it in the bookstores and it was out there. They were on Oprah and all that. That's not new. Very old. Yeah, like turn of the century. Right. And so there's people from turn of the century, Edmund Schaffsbury, Emmett Fox. They are the generation of these people who then gave us Napoleon Hill and behind that Norman Vincent Peel and you know, so there's a genealogy and all this. And it's all to this concept of magnetism. Power of attraction. Of how do you attract people? Right. You know, how do you attract people, opportunity, wealth, et cetera, to you, making yourself magnetic to them. Right. And the heritage of that with very practical direct response strategies that are designed to do the same thing that are designed to create at least the perception, if not the reality that the prospective customer client patient donor is discovering you and by their own decision initiative moving to you rather than having stuff pushed on them or being pursued and chased and hunted down and drugged back to the cave. Right. And it changes the whole positioning, not only of your marketing, but as you as the business owner too, doesn't it? Well, it changes. It does a lot. Yeah. It changes positioning. It changes the nature, the dynamic of the relationship. Right. And now it allows for expert positioning, which the other tends to denigrate it. Right. Right. I always say no heart surgeon makes cold calls. Right. So you will never have a cardiac surgeon. Not enough. Knock on your door with a sample case. You'll never have a cardiac surgeon. Offer to do a demo. You'll never have a, you probably won't have a demo offer. You'll never have a cardiac surgeon calling you at five o'clock at night. So how do you want to be perceived? So there's an issue of positioning, which then links too often to price. Okay. Because price resistance and price elasticity goes up and down based on the dynamic of the relationship. Right. There's acceptance. Right. And again, if you used a cardiac surgeon analogy, if he says to you today, Dave, look, see this? Right. Lie down. You got to get into the operatory and we got to put in a stent. Right. Nine out of 10 people. We're going to do it. Strip naked, jump out of the cart, say let's go. Right. Right. If the pharmacist over at Rite Aid, right, or, well, let's go further. Let's take somebody who is in pursuit. So the pharmacist is in pursuit. Let's take pursuit. So if you're an herbal life distributor, no disrespect meant herbal life. But if you're an herbal life distributor says, Dave, you need a heart operation. Right. You say, maybe I'll get a second to pay you. Right. So it affects that. It affects acceptance of prescription, if you will. It affects price elasticity versus price resistance. It affects referrals after the fact because often people who are pursued or pushed may buy and may even wind up happy with what they bought. They don't like the process. But they are so uncomfortable with the process that got them there that they are reluctant to stick anybody else in it. So it affects all of these things. So the other thing that it's beautiful for is it ends cold calling. I remember when I first got started, getting on the phone. They're all dead. That's probably the ugliest. It's awful. It's awful. It's awful. It totally, it eliminates that and has people coming to you. So cold prospecting, true cold prospecting, is the lowest level of work there is in the marketing slash sales field. Right. You fall off that cliff into janitorial work and that's as low as you can go. It's as dumb and brute force of a use of your time as you could possibly engage it. And stupid sales managers still to this day take potentially good salespeople and they put them into doing that and in most cases they kill them. They destroy them. Only super desperate or super strong persons survive that experience. Right. Because it is so horrid. Right. And far more horrid today than it was when you were I did. I mean, so in 1968 I was making cold phone calls to consumers out of the white pages with the, we want to do a free water analysis and survey in your house. Right. In my case, not to sell water softwares, but to sell amway soap. And you're trying to get an appointment to come in and do a water analysis with somebody you've called cold out of the white pages. Your only means of differentiation is the ability to pronounce the name versus soap. By the way, if you don't want to get a lot of cold calls, it's good to be like Russian or Polish or, you know. But in the late 60s, A, you still had a fair amount of people at home during the day and a fairly significant number of them would actually engage and talk to you. You at least had a shot. Today, they resent that call. They thought they had barriers in place for that call. Yes. And knocking on doors and the equivalent of it networking, you know, going to the event where I sort of pin you between the wall and the potted palm and give you my elevator speech and force my business card into your hand, which is push. Right. And I can get yours so I can pursue. Right. So all that is just horrible. It's like hole mining, but it's bad. And magnetic marketing for sales professionals replaces it. Right. It absolutely ends it. Right. Talk about the famous going from a knowing past to welcome guests. Well, so that really is, so do you want the story or you want the story? Tell the story. It's a great story. I'll tell it short and unfunny because we don't want to tax her. No. No. Right. So it's ancient history now, but it's when I lived in Phoenix, which by the way is warm, unlike where we are here. And in the summer, it's as hot as it is cold here, brutal. And I'm home alone and I'm a kitchen counter, coffee, phone, clients, and a very persistent person who I immediately categorize as a past is ringing the doorbell and then ignored knocking on the door. Your options of who can be at your door unexpected and uninvited on a weekday in the middle of the day in a residential neighborhood are fairly narrow and predictable. Right. So our joke was it was either the Avon lady or the Jehovah's Witnesses, they carpooled. They all come in one car and then the Avon people go to one end of the street, the Jehovah Witnesses go to the other. When they're done, they get back in the car and they go to the donut shop. I got a complaint letter about that by the way from an Avon sales manager. That's a Jehovah's Witness. No, what you would expect. A big complaint from an Avon sales manager that I was denigrating there, ruining their self-esteem with this joke. But anyway, so this guy is ringing a bell and banging and banging and I'm ignoring and ignoring and ignoring because I'm a stubborn guy. And eventually he has climbed over a fence, a mason wall kind of thing with shards of glass in the top of it to discourage people from doing this, come past the pool and the back yard and so on. And he is now at the patio doors through which he can see me. You're thrilled. He's banging away at the patio doors. Almost impossible to ignore but I manage it for four, five, six minutes before I finally give in. The reason he's there, he's a good Samaritan who has stopped because the front of my house is on fire. So some moron, which at this point it's very hard to accuse anybody else of being a moron, but so some moron driving through the neighborhood has undoubtedly flicked a cigarette and it has landed in 110 degrees, zero humidity, front landscaping and now the flames are up the front of the house and across the wood trim and it's not good. And this guy thinks I'm a complete idiot, flaming idiot. Are you married? Really? To whom? Is that person here? No. Doesn't hang out with you much, does he? That's like the worst laugh. She's a lovely person but the laugh is just deadly. And she's really glassy cake. No question about that. I mean that laugh is just frightening. So we did tell her to laugh. Yeah, I've growing list of regrets the older I get. So this guy who thinks I'm an idiot, which arguable, is now back out there, get the hose and call 911 and all that. So what's significant is that in 30 seconds he went from being the most annoying pest ever to visit the Kennedy household to being one of the most welcome guests ever to visit the Kennedy household. And why is that? Because he had a message that was personally relevant, therefore welcomed and on top of that it was well timed. Now timing is difficult to get right but not impossible. So the whole prospect selection part of magnetic marketing includes the possibility of getting timing right and we get smarter and smarter and smarter about that and his privacy gets more and more elusive. It's more and more possible to do it but even without timing there's a difference between a welcome message for a welcome audience versus an unwelcome message. So one part of being magnetic is what we call message to market match. Right. So let's talk about that because you've talked about something called the marketing triangle. What is this marketing triangle? Three elements. So picture of three-legged stool, any leg too short, hard to balance a glass of water or your butt on it, a leg missing entirely, not going to work. Arguably no one leg is more important than the other and you can really approach them in any order although there is a best order in most situations. And so the three sides of the triangle or the three legs of the stool, whichever metal image you want, are message, what do we say, market, who do we say it to and by definition who do we not say it to. And media, how do we take the message and deliver it to the person it's intended for in a way that will be effective. So climbing over my fence and banging on my back door to tell me about the latest and greatest mattress is not going to be effective. Right. It will get you shot if the person owns a gun. Right. And in Texas that's legal. How long we've been up here? We only have five hours of this. My eyes are bleeding already. But there with the message, your house is on fire, all of a sudden you're welcome, right? Right. Every thing has to be fit together in a way that is effective. And for the most part you see people not putting the pieces together. So they create a message with no thought of market and they may have a great message but they're delivering it to people that for whom it is a bad fit. Right. They have selected great prospects but now they have a terrible message for them. Or they're using a means of delivering it that is either inappropriate for the message, can't deliver it in an effective way, or is inappropriate for the audience it's being delivered to because they don't like getting information and opportunities in that way. Right. And then if you really could put a circle around the triangle too and put timing if you wanted to get everything exactly right, couldn't you? Yeah. Because you get your message, market have a circle around it. And if you put a square around it then you'd have Creskins ESP Act. It's actually the circle of the triangle. Close enough. Nobody else has any idea what we're talking about there. No idea. No idea. So when you start to work with this triangle, what we're really talking about is creating a great deal of magnetism by having a message that is extremely magnetic to a specific and particular audience. And then delivering it in a way that is appropriate for them and that they are known to be responsive to. Fantastic. So one of the things that we have literally thousands of folks that are watching this right now, they're saying, well, what is different about this version of magnetic marketing over the other versions? Because there have been many versions of magnetic marketing. The key that makes the magnetic marketing system different is all these little colored posters. No, those are from my use. You will not be getting those post-it notes. Is the toolkit. Put that on the screen. Post-it notes down. The toolkit. This is what makes magnetic marketing different than really anything else that's out there. This includes templates for every business category that there is. So for the owners of magnetic marketing, first of all, the big mistake that people make down when they go through magnetic marketing is they only go to the section of, okay, I'm in business to business. So I'm just going to use that. That kind of goes back to one of the biggest marketing mistakes we've talked about. It's another marketing mistake. The great deal that people make is I call it being Amish. Right. Being Amish? Yeah. So most people are very provincial, very narrow-minded, very Amish about the business that they are in. And they, so the Amish community is a closed community, right? And everybody does exactly what everybody else does. By rule and by custom. And because that's the only thing they look at. Right? And so most people in businesses do the same thing. So all the dentists look at what all the other dentists are doing. And if a guy came out of dental school and became a dentist and most of the dentists had no electricity, he probably wouldn't get electricity either. Right. You know, they're very Amish and they're very resistant to anything from outside. They're a little closed community. And that resistance is very harmful because all breakthroughs come from outside, not inside. There's almost never an organic internal breakthrough in any business industry or profession. They all come from outside. And your leverage comes from being able to do something clever and cool and effective that not every competitor is doing. And by the time they catch up to that, you've got another one. Right. Right? Well, you can't do that by being one of eight in a circle looking at each other. Right. You can only do that if you're willing to break the circle. Right. And so the point is that you want, you're going to want to go through all of the sections here. And for people who are asking, well, how is this different than previous versions? Well, all of the examples are brand spanking new in here. And then of course, 25 years ago, there wasn't the internet. There was no internet component that wasn't a media that was available. So now for all of the copyright free documents that are in here in each section, there are internet copyright free examples. So meaning that copyright free is really important to this system as well. Meaning that all you need to do is take it and you can use it basically as is just by taking a few words because they're basically templates that you can take and use. You can have it for emails, banner ads, tweets, text messages, all different kinds of marketing materials are in here. And so this is really a new tool kit, updated tool kit to reflect the new types of media that came on board. Go ahead, Doug. Well, yeah. With that said, I would make two important points. One is actually the first version of magnetic marketing was done before you actually had broadcast fax. So if you want to go back far enough, you know, so our our portfolio of available media continues to grow and grow and grow and grow. One mistake people make is thinking that there's OB to be jettisoned and new media to use, which is a mistake. But the sort of the other side of that coin that I think is important is all the magnetic marketing tools and templates are designed and engineered. Not only to be easy to use, but to cross media platforms, right? And to create as much evergreen as you can. So a big distinction between what a lot of people how a lot of people approach marketing is that most people do it. It is a thing to do. I'm going to do some marketing for my business this month, meaning I'm going to put some stuff together and I'm going to get an ad in or I'm going to, you know, and often that's driven by desperation. It used to be driven by like the media sales guy showing up in your business, but pretty much that doesn't happen anymore. So now it's pretty much driven by desperation. It's like random and erratic acts. This is about, in many cases, creating marketing assets. Meaning that you use the tools and the templates and you create something once and it is able to be used continuously in rotation or periodically over a long, long, long, long, long period of time. I forgot to grab it. I meant to grab it up my desk this morning. So I'm not going to get the numbers exactly right. But one of our members, Keith Lee, you know. So Keith, out of magnetic marketing, I'm not going to get this precise now, so, but he'll forgive me, roughly 22 years ago for his first business. He now has three, a B2B and a very small B2B business specialized in an inch. Took a campaign out of magnetic marketing filled in the blanks, you know, fixed it up a little to be for his business. He has used it every single year for 22 years. Has not changed it a lick, except in the most recent years there is an option of responding by going through a website. Sure, right. But other than that, it's the same campaign that was created 22 years ago. Right. Every year at a particular time it comes off the shelf, it gets put into the mail. It brings in roughly $14 for every dollar that is spent on sending it out. In direct revenue, he's counted almost $2 million from it. In indirect revenue, meaning the back end value of the new customer acquired with it who then stays, which has stacked over 22 years. We're talking about $20 or $30 million, probably against the $2 million of front end value. And that's a very different experience than your Google AdWords campaign this week, that by next week all the algorithms will have changed. Right. All the other bidders will have affected it. Right. And basically you'll be reinventing everything all over again. Correct. That's not to say you shouldn't do Google AdWords. That is my point. My point is that there's a difference between doing marketing and creating marketing assets. And creating marketing systems. Well, this is really about that. Right. So this is about creating a marketing system. And so we've got so much to share with you here. You now understand the foundational principles of magnetic marketing. This was just the foundations.
